name: dumbbell one arm snatch
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand with an overhand grip.
Bend your knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up.
Lower the dumbbell towards the ground, keeping it close to your body.
Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles, driving the dumbbell upwards in a straight line.
As the dumbbell reaches shoulder height, quickly rotate your hand and punch it overhead, fully extending your arm.
Catch the dumbbell overhead with a slight bend in your knees and hips.
Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch to the other arm.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one hand with an overhand grip. Bend your knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up. Lower the dumbbell towards the ground, keeping it close to your body. Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles, driving the dumbbell upwards in a straight line. As the dumbbell reaches shoulder height, quickly rotate your hand and punch it overhead, fully extending your arm. Catch the dumbbell overhead with a slight bend in your knees and hips. Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position in a controlled manner. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch to the other arm.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
